question.label: Self-read opener
question.name: Before I share my view, how do you think the last stretch has gone?
question.description: Anchors the conversation in their self-assessment first; gap vs manager's view becomes a signal.

answer: I can name wins, but I am not always sure they match the team bar.
note: He can identify wins but explicitly names uncertainty about whether they meet the team bar, which is a strong clarity gap with mild engagement signal.

question.label: Team bar mismatch
question.name: When you say 'team bar' — what feels unclear about it?
question.description: Drills into the specific source of his uncertainty about standards before moving on.

answer: Feedback has helped, though I still miss consistency under time pressure.
note: He says feedback helped but he still cannot hold the quality bar consistently under time pressure, which is a mild ongoing clarity gap.

question.label: Time pressure consistency
question.name: Under time pressure, what specifically starts to slip in your work?
question.description: Drills into the named consistency problem so the gap is concrete before the arc moves on.

answer: I sometimes focus on polish and lose sight of the core message.
note: He names a specific clarity failure under pressure: prioritising polish over the core message.

question.label: Recent example
question.name: Can you walk me through one piece of recent work that you think met the bar?
question.description: Forces a concrete example of quality rather than a general claim.

answer: I want clearer examples of what excellent junior-level work looks like here.
note: He did not provide a recent example and instead asked for clearer examples of excellent junior-level work, signaling strong role-quality ambiguity and mild passivity.

question.label: Excellent examples
question.name: What examples of excellent junior-level work would make the bar clearer for you?
question.description: Drills into the specific examples he says are missing so the evidence-stage ambiguity is concrete.

answer: When reviews are broad, I am not sure what to change first.
note: He names a specific clarity problem in reviews — broad feedback leaves him unable to prioritise what to change first; wellbeing signal is mildly negative through review overwhelm.

question.label: Broad reviews gap
question.name: When feedback is broad, what kind of direction is missing most?
question.description: Names the specific gap inside review feedback before moving on in the arc.

answer: I would like to grow faster in concept rationale, not just execution speed.
note: Names a specific missing direction in feedback — stronger guidance on concept rationale over execution speed — which is a strong clarity gap; also reveals a growth thread worth probing next.

question.label: Expectation mismatch
question.name: Where do your standards for good work differ from mine right now?
question.description: Names a likely source of misalignment without softening the gap.

answer: A tighter loop on one skill at a time would help me improve.
note: Deficiency-as-request: he names a missing tighter feedback loop on one skill at a time, signaling strong clarity and growth gaps with mild disengagement; [THREAD-DEFERRED-WINDDOWN] 'one skill at a time' specificity should be picked up next session.

question.label: Blocked practice
question.name: What part of concept rationale feels hardest to improve right now?
question.description: Surfaces the specific growth block inside concept rationale rather than a vague development wish.

answer: If we align on concrete criteria, I can work against them confidently.
note: Names missing concrete criteria as the blocker to improving concept rationale, which is a strong growth deficit and mild clarity gap.

question.label: Next step
question.name: What is the first concrete change you want to make before we meet again?
question.description: Ends on an actionable commitment tied to the discussion.

note: [SKIP] no signal — planner bypassed, queue carried forward
